prompt:
Our nightly drop script `backup.zsh` (repo root, zsh 5.9) does its one job fine: `zsh backup.zsh <src-dir> <dest-dir>` walks the source with zsh globbing (`**/*(.N)` — plain files only, dotfiles stay behind), copies every file to the same relative path under the destination, writes `manifest.txt` there (one relative path per line, byte order), and reports `files copied: <n>`. Keep every bit of that working — the EXISTING BEHAVIOR block in `test_backup.zsh` is green today and must stay green, byte for byte.

What it can't do yet, and what I need this sprint: the drops are drowning in cache junk and log noise, and I want to preview a drop before it burns disk.

1. Selection flags, repeatable, given before the two paths: `--include <pat>` and `--exclude <pat>`. A pattern is a zsh extended glob matched against each candidate's RELATIVE path as one string — apply the argv word with the ${~} expansion flag under setopt extended_glob, never eval, never let it word-split. Know that matching context cold: in a string match `*` crosses `/`, so `*.log` catches a log three directories down and `cache/*` covers that whole subtree; there is no special `**` there (`**` is just `*` twice), and (#i) case-insensitive patterns must work. With no includes every file is a candidate; with includes a file must match at least one of them. Excludes always win — a file matching any exclude stays home even when an include wanted it.

2. `--dry-run`: print the plan instead of executing it — one line per would-copy file, `copy<TAB><relative path>`, same byte order the manifest would use, exit 0, and touch NOTHING: no destination directory, no writes anywhere (the harness snapshots the source tree with checksums and asserts the destination doesn't exist afterwards). No summary line in this mode; an empty plan is empty output and still exit 0.

Bookkeeping that has to line up: a filtered real run copies only the selected files and `manifest.txt` lists exactly those; the `files copied: <n>` summary counts that same set; `--include`/`--exclude` without a value, or any flag the script doesn't know, is a usage error, exit 64, and the usage line grows to mention the new flags (exact bytes are pinned in the tests). The walk stays zsh-native — glob qualifiers, no find, no rsync.

`zsh test_backup.zsh` from the workspace root is the acceptance gate; the INCLUDE / EXCLUDE and DRY-RUN blocks in it define the feature. Don't touch the test file.
